text: You stand up from this kitchen table
after breakfast. You reach for your
coffee mug and suddenly the room tilts,
not dramatically, just enough to make
you grab the counter. Your heart skips.
You wait a second and then it passes.
You shake it off and tell yourself it's
just a little dizzy spell. Maybe you
stood up too fast. Maybe you didn't
sleep well. Maybe this is just what
getting older feels like. But here's
what I want you to hear right now. That
moment at the kitchen counter is not
just aging. It is your body quietly
sending you a signal. And today we're
going to show you exactly what that
signal means. And more importantly, what
one simple stretch against your wall can
do about it. I am so glad we are talking
about this today because what you just
described that that brief tilt, that
grab for the counter, happens to
millions of people over 60 and the vast
majority of them never find out why.
They accept it. They slow down. They
start avoiding certain movements. And
slowly, without even realizing it, their
world gets a little smaller. But it
doesn't have to. The science is very
clear on this. The body can be
retrained. The dizziness can be reduced.
And in many cases, it can be
dramatically improved with something as
simple as standing against a wall for a
few minutes each day.
I'm Dr. Howard Tucker. I've spent
decades working with older adults,
studying longevity, studying what
separates the people who thrive in their
70s and 80s from those who quietly
retreat from life. And dizziness is one
of the most underestimated obstacles I
have seen in my career. It affects
balance. It affects confidence. It
affects whether someone reaches for that
coffee mug with ease or with fear. My
guest today has thought deeply about
this for most of his life. He is Dr. Dr.
Shagaki Hinohara, a Japanese physician
and longevity expert whose work on aging
well into later life has influenced
doctors and patients around the world.
Welcome Dr. Hinhara.
Thank you Howard. It is always a
pleasure to think through these
questions together. And I want to say
something to everyone listening before
we go further. If you have experienced
dizziness and you have been told it is
simply a part of getting older, I want
to gently challenge that idea. The aging
body is remarkable. It adapts. It
responds. And the vestibular system, the
inner ear system that controls your
sense of balance is far more trainable
than most people realize. That is what
today is about exactly. So here is what
we are going to walk through in this
conversation.
First, why dizzy spells after 60 are so
widely misunderstood
and why that misunderstanding keeps
people stuck. Second, what is actually
happening inside the body when those
spells occur. And third, the one wall
stretch backed by solid evidence that
has helped countless seniors
dramatically reduce how often dizziness
disrupts their day. So, let's get into
it and I want to give people something
useful in the very first few minutes.
Dr. Hinhara, before we even get to the
science, is there one thing people can
do right now, today, if they are
struggling with this? Yes, and it is
simple enough to start now as they
watch. Stand with your back flat against
a wall, feet about 6 in away from the
base of the wall. Let the back of your
head, your upper back, and your hips all
make gentle contact with the wall
surface.
Hold that position for 30 seconds.
Breathe slowly. Then tuck your chin very
slightly. Just a small gentle movement
so that the back of your neck lengthens
toward the wall. That is it for now.
That small position teaches your nervous
system something it may have forgotten.
We will come back to the full version
later in this video. But that first
contact back against the wall, head
gently touching is where everything
begins. And if someone does only that
today, they have already started. I love
that you started there because I think
for a lot of seniors the barrier is not
information. It is the belief that
nothing can be done. And I've seen this
in my own practice. Someone comes in
reporting dizziness. They've had it for
months, sometimes years. And when I ask
why they waited so long, the answer is
almost always the same. I thought it was
just my age. So let's talk about why
that belief is so common and why it is
incomplete. The misunderstanding begins
with how dizziness is talked about or
rather not talked about. When a younger
person gets dizzy, there is alarm. Tests
are run. Causes are investigated. But
when a person over 60 reports dizziness,
it is often minimized, normalized,
filed under expected changes. And so the
person internalizes that message. They
stop mentioning it. They start
compensating. They grip furniture more.
They avoid turning their head quickly.
They sit down more often. All of these
things are natural responses to feeling
unsteady. But none of them address the
root cause. And over time, avoiding
movement actually makes the vestibular
system weaker, not stronger.
The body needs movement to maintain
balance. When we stop moving, the system
that keeps us steady loses its
calibration.
That is such an important point. The
avoidance creates a cycle. Less movement
leads to weaker balance signals which
leads to more dizziness which leads to
even less movement. And meanwhile the
person believes this is all just aging.
So they never step off that cycle. But
what is actually going on in the body?
Because I think when people understand
what is physically happening, it removes
a lot of the fear and with less fear
there is more willingness to try
something.
Absolutely. And understanding is the
beginning of action. You are not alone
in this. You are not imagining it. And
you are not powerless. That is the
message I want to carry all the way
through our conversation today.
So let's talk about the science in plain
language. What is actually going on
inside the body when a senior
experiences those brief dizzy spells?
Let me give you two pictures to hold in
your mind. The first is a snow globe.
Inside your inner ear, there are tiny
calcium crystals, almost like grains of
sand that sit on a soft membrane. When
you move your head, those crystals
shift, and that movement sends signals
to your brain, telling it where your
head is in space. It is one of the
body's most elegant systems.
But after 60, those crystals become less
dense. They loosen more easily and
sometimes they drift into the
fluid-filled canals of the inner ear
where they do not belong.
When that happens, even a small
movement, tilting your head, rolling
over in bed, standing up quickly sends a
confused signal to the brain. The brain
gets two different messages at the same
time. One from the eyes, one from the
inner ear.
And that mismatch is experienced as
dizziness or a spinning sensation.
This is called BPPV,
benign paroxismal positional vertigo.
And by age 70, research suggests that
one in three people will have
experienced it at least once.
And BPPV is not dangerous. Is that
right? The word benign is important
there. But it is certainly disruptive.
Very disruptive. And it is important to
say benign means it is not caused by
something sinister like a stroke or a
tumor. But benign does not mean minor.
Dizziness is one of the leading
contributors to falls in older adults.
Fools are one of the most serious health
risks for people over 65.
So while BPPV is not dangerous in
itself, the cascade it can trigger
absolutely is. This is why we take it
seriously and this is why a simple
consistent stretch, one that gently
retrains the relationship between the
neck, the inner ear and the brain can
have such a meaningful impact on quality
of life.
You mentioned the neck and that brings
me to the second cause you often talk
about. Not everyone with dizziness has
BPPV. Correct. There is another pathway
that runs right through the cervical
spine.
Yes. And this one is even less wellnown.
The second picture I want you to hold is
of a telephone exchange, an
old-fashioned switchboard. Your cervical
spine, the seven bones at the top of
your neck, is covered in small, highly
sensitive receptors. These receptors are
constantly sending information to your
brain about the position of your head.
They are part of the same conversation
as your inner ear. They work together.
But here is the problem.
As we age, many of us develop tightness
and stiffness in the neck. The muscles
shorten. The posture shifts forward.
Many seniors spend hours each day
looking down at a phone or a tablet or
sitting in a chair that rounds the
shoulders forward. This forward head
position where the chin juts ahead of
the chest distorts the signals coming
from those neck receptors. The
switchboard starts sending the wrong
messages and the brain receiving
conflicting input from the inner ear and
the neck at the same time may say
experiences this as dizziness or a
floating offbalance feeling. Researchers
call this cervviccoenic dizziness.
dizziness that originates from the
cervical spine. And studies published in
medical journals have found that
addressing cervical posture and neck
muscle function can significantly reduce
or eliminate this type of dizziness. And
this is where the wall stretch becomes
so powerful because it addresses both
pathways at once, doesn't it? It gently
repositions the head, lengthens the
neck, and retrains the posture that may
have been contributing to those confused
signals for months or even years.
Precisely. And this is why I describe it
not as an exercise in the traditional
sense. It is a recalibration.
You are giving your brain a cleaner,
more accurate signal. And the brain is
very good at adapting when you give it
the right input. This is the remarkable
thing about the nervous system. At any
age, it retains the ability to learn.
That capacity does not disappear after
60. It simply needs the right
invitation. Let's get into the full
stretch. Now, walk us through it step by
step. And I want to remind everyone
listening, you may want to find a wall
right now or pause and come back to this
part with a pen and paper because this
is the heart of today's conversation.
Let us go through it carefully. And I
want to say at the start this stretch is
gentle. It is safe for most people. But
if you have a known neck injury,
cervical stenosis or any condition
affecting your spine, please check with
your own doctor before beginning. That
guidance applies to everything we
discussed today. Your doctor knows your
history and that conversation matters.
Absolutely. Please do talk with your own
doctor. This is general guidance, not
personal medical advice. Go ahead, Dr.
Hinhara.
The first position is what I call the
wall reset. Stand with your back against
a smooth wall. Your heels can be about 4
to 6 in from the base of the wall. This
helps your lower back find a more
natural curve. Now, gently press the
back of your head against the wall. Not
forcefully. Think of it as a light,
deliberate contact. Your shoulders will
naturally move back and down as you do
this. Your chest will open slightly.
Take three slow breaths here. Notice
what you feel. Many people, especially
those who have been carrying forward
head posture for a long time, will feel
a mild stretch at the back of the neck,
between the shoulder blades, or across
the chest. That sensation is normal.
That is your body remembering a position
it may not have visited in years.
I want to pause there because
I think that is a powerful image. The
body remembering a position. It is not
forcing anything new. It is restoring
something that was always there.
That is exactly right. The second part
of the stretch builds on that. With your
head still gently touching the wall,
tuck your chin very slightly downward
and inward as if you are making a very
small dignified nod,
just a centimeter or two.
This movement lengthens the muscles at
the back of the cervical spine and
brings the head into a more neutral
alignment over the shoulders. Hold this
position for 10 seconds. Release. Repeat
three times. Over days and weeks, this
movement teaches the deep neck muscles
to support the head in proper alignment.
And as those muscles strengthen and the
posture corrects, the confused signals
from the cervical receptors begin to
quiet down.
And what about the inner earpiece, the
BPPV pathway? Does the wall stretch
address that as well? It does through a
third component. After you have done the
wall reset and the chin tuck, while
still standing with your back against
the wall, very slowly and gently turn
your head to the right, just 10 to 15°.
Hold for 5 seconds. Return to center.
Then slowly turn to the left. 10 to 15°.
Hold for 5 seconds. Return to center.
These small controlled head movements
while your body is stabilized against
the wall allow the vestibular system to
practice processing positional change in
a safe supported environment.
You are giving the brain gentle movement
data good data in a context where you
cannot fall where you feel secure and
where the input is clear and controlled.
Over time, this retrains the vestibular
systems response to movement. The
dizziness that once accompanied head
turns begins to diminish. How long does
someone need to do this each day? And
how quickly might they expect to notice
a difference?
The full sequence, wall reset, chin
tuck, and gentle head turns takes
approximately 3 to 4 minutes. I
recommend doing it twice a day.
Once in the morning before you begin
your day and once in the evening before
sitting down for the night. Consistency
is everything here. This is not a
one-day remedy. It is a small daily step
repeated regularly that builds
cumulative benefit. Most people who
practice this faithfully report a
noticeable reduction in dizzy spells
within 2 to 3 weeks. Some experience
improvement within days. And the people
who stay with it for 60 days or more
often describe a fundamental shift in
how steady and confident they feel on
their feet. This is not a miracle. It is
physiology. You are giving your nervous
system exactly what it needs to
recalibrate and the nervous system
responds. I want to add one practical
point here for anyone who might feel
unsteady during that head turn portion.
You are against the wall. That is your
safety net. If at any point you feel a
strong dizzy sensation, stop the
movement. Press gently into the wall.
Fix your gaze on one still point in
front of you and breathe slowly. The
feeling will pass and you can pick up
where you left off the next day. The
body adapts at its own pace, and that
pace deserves respect. Well said. And
one more thing I want to highlight. This
stretch is not just for people who
already have dizziness.
If you are over 60 and you are watching
this as a preventive measure, this is
one of the most valuable habits you can
build. Cervical stiffness and forward
head posture develop gradually, often
before dizziness symptoms appear.
Starting this practice now means you are
staying ahead of the curve. You are
maintaining the communication pathways
between your neck, your inner ear, and
your brain. And that maintenance pays
dividends for years.
Let's talk about building this into real
life because I know for a lot of people
the challenge isn't the 3 minutes. It's
remembering to do it and believing it's
worth continuing even on days when
progress feels invisible. That is the
honest truth about any health practice.
Progress with the vestibular system is
quiet. You do not feel it rebuilding.
You simply one day notice that you stood
up from the table and nothing tilted.
You reached for something on a high
shelf and your balance held. You walked
across the kitchen and felt completely
steady. These are small moments, but
they add up to a life that is larger and
freer than before, and that is worth 3
or 4 minutes a day. To anchor the habit,
I suggest pairing it with something you
already do consistently. Do your wall
stretch while the kettle is boiling for
morning tea. Do it while your evening
news is starting. Do not wait for the
perfect moment. Pair it with an existing
ritual and it becomes part of the day
without requiring willpower.
That habit pairing idea is so practical
and so underused. And I want to speak to
the caregivers who may be listening
today as well. If you have a parent or a
loved one over 60 who experiences dizzy
spells, this is something you can do
with them. Stand at the wall together.
Make it a shared 2 minutes in the
morning. The emotional safety of doing
it with someone they trust makes a real
difference, especially for older adults
who feel nervous about movement. That is
a beautiful point. The body heals faster
in a context of safety and calm. There
is genuine physiology behind that. When
we are relaxed, the nervous system is
more receptive.
When we are tense or fearful, the
vestibular signals become harder to
retrain.
So do this in a quiet moment. No rush,
no pressure, just a wall, a few minutes,
and the quiet decision to give your body
something good. And for anyone
wondering, is this all I need to do? The
honest answer is that the wall stretch
is a powerful starting point, but a full
picture of balance health after 60 also
includes staying hydrated. Dehydration
is a surprisingly common and overlooked
cause of dizziness in older adults. It
includes getting enough sleep because
the vestibular system consolidates and
recalibrates during deep sleep. And it
includes regular movement in general,
gentle walks, light activity because a
moving body maintains its balance
calibration far better than a sedentary
one. These are not complicated things.
They are small daily steps practiced
with intention. And I would add if your
dizziness is severe, frequent, or
accompanied by other symptoms such as
headache, vision changes, hearing loss,
or difficulty speaking. Please do not
rely on a podcast alone. See your
doctor. These symptoms deserve careful
evaluation. The wall stretch is for the
common persistent posture related and
mild vestibular dizziness that so many
seniors experience quietly every day.
For anything beyond that, your own
doctor is your first and best resource.
As we bring today's conversation to a
close, I want to leave everyone with one
clear action tonight. Before you sit
down for the evening, find a smooth wall
in your home. Stand with your back
against it. Let your head touch gently.
Take three slow breaths. Feel what it is
like to be tall and supported and
upright. That is your starting point.
That is the first conversation between
your brain and your body that something
is about to change. You don't need to do

against the wall, breathing slowly. That
is enough to begin. And I want to say
with great warmth and sincerity to
everyone listening, this is not just
aging. What you feel in those dizzy

And the fact that you are here

that is itself an act of tremendous

The people who age with the most

The relationship between sleep quality
